perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include information that: i. is or becomes part of the public domain through no fault or breach on the part of Receiving Party; ii. was previously known to the Receiving Party free of any obligation to keep it confidential, as evidenced by its business records; iii. is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party; or iv. is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use of any Confidential Information, as evidenced by its records.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include information that: i. is or becomes part of the public domain through no fault or breach on the part of Receiving Party; ii. was previously known to the Receiving Party free of any obligation to keep it confidential, as evidenced by its business records; iii. is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party; iv. is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use of any Confidential Information, as evidenced by its records; or v. information that the Disclosing Party marks as "Not Confidential" after disclosure, regardless of its initial classification.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds a condition where the Disclosing Party can retroactively mark information as "Not Confidential" even if it was initially considered confidential, creating an in-text contradiction in the same section, Section 2.2. This contradicts the general definition of Confidential Information, which assumes the Disclosing Party designates information as confidential *at the time of disclosure or beforehand.* The change creates uncertainty about what truly constitutes Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information shall not include information that: i. is or becomes part of the public domain through no fault or breach on the part of Receiving Party; ii. was previously known to the Receiving Party free of any obligation to keep it confidential, as evidenced by its business records; iii. is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a third party; iv. is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use of any Confidential Information, as evidenced by its records; or v. information that the Disclosing Party marks as "Not Confidential" after disclosure, regardless of its initial classification.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a condition where the Disclosing Party can retroactively mark information as 'Not Confidential', creating an in-text contradiction. This contradicts the general definition of Confidential Information, which assumes the Disclosing Party designates information as confidential at the time of disclosure or beforehand. The change creates uncertainty about what truly constitutes Confidential Information, as it allows for a change in classification after the initial disclosure. The contradicted text is the same section as the changed text, which makes the contradiction very obvious and impactful.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall only use Confidential Information for the purpose of negotiation and/or evaluation of any co-operation between the Parties. The Parties shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party, other than to their officers, employees or consultants (engaged in the matter of this Agreement) on a need to know basis, without the prior written approval of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall only use Confidential Information for the purpose of negotiation and/or evaluation of any co-operation between the Parties. The Parties may disclose Confidential Information to any third party, other than direct competitors, without prior written approval. The Parties shall not disclose Confidential Information to any direct competitor, other than to their officers, employees or consultants (engaged in the matter of this Agreement) on a need to know basis, without the prior written approval of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text strictly prohibits disclosing confidential information to third parties without approval. The modified text allows disclosure to *any* third party, except direct competitors. This creates a direct contradiction in the same section, 2.4, regarding the restrictions on disclosing Confidential Information to third parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Parties shall only use Confidential Information for the purpose of negotiation and/or evaluation of any co-operation between the Parties. The Parties may disclose Confidential Information to any third party, other than direct competitors, without prior written approval. The Parties shall not disclose Confidential Information to any direct competitor, other than to their officers, employees or consultants (engaged in the matter of this Agreement) on a need to know basis, without the prior written approval of the other Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 2.4 to allow the Parties to disclose Confidential Information to any third party, except direct competitors, without prior written approval. This directly contradicts the initial clause in the original text and, more importantly, in the Contradicted Text (which is the same as the Changed Text), which states that the Parties shall only use Confidential Information for the purpose of negotiation and/or evaluation of any co-operation between the Parties and shall not disclose Confidential Information to any third party without prior written approval. This change introduces a clear and impactful contradiction within the document regarding the disclosure of confidential information, meeting the criteria for a strong in-text contradiction under Structural Flaws.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party shall not be entitled to copy any Confidential Information furnished by the Disclosing Party hereunder, unless and to the extent it is necessary for the purpose of this Agreement. Upon request of the Disclosing Party or upon the termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall return, delete or destroy (as instructed in writing by the Disclosing Party) all Confidential Information, and all copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party, unless otherwise agreed in writing. The Receiving Party shall be entitled to copy any Confidential Information furnished by the Disclosing Party hereunder, even if it is not necessary for the purpose of this Agreement. Upon request of the Disclosing Party or upon the termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party may, at its sole discretion, return, delete or destroy (as instructed in writing by the Disclosing Party) all Confidential Information, and all copies thereof.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces multiple contradictions within section 3.1. First, it makes the ownership of confidential information conditional ("unless otherwise agreed in writing"), contradicting the initial statement of the Disclosing Party's exclusive ownership. Second, it allows the Receiving Party to copy confidential information for any reason, negating the restriction to only what's necessary for the agreement. Finally, it makes the return/deletion/destruction of information optional, violating the original requirement to do so upon request or termination.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party shall remain the property of the Disclosing Party, unless otherwise agreed in writing. The Receiving Party shall be entitled to copy any Confidential Information furnished by the Disclosing Party hereunder, even if it is not necessary for the purpose of this Agreement. Upon request of the Disclosing Party or upon the termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party may, at its sole discretion, return, delete or destroy (as instructed in writing by the Disclosing Party) all Confidential Information, and all copies thereof.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces multiple contradictions as explained in the 'Perturbation Explanation'. The original text required the Receiving Party to return, delete, or destroy confidential information upon request or termination. The changed text makes this optional ('may, at its sole discretion'), directly contradicting the previous mandatory requirement. The contradictory text and Changed text are the same.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information (including but not limited to all intellectual property rights emanating from or related to Confidential Information) remains the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party and nothing in this Agreement grants any right or license in the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information (including but not limited to all intellectual property rights emanating from or related to Confidential Information) remains the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party, except for any improvements or derivative works created by the Receiving Party using the Confidential Information, which shall be jointly owned. Nothing in this Agreement grants any right or license in the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party, unless a separate agreement is reached regarding licensing terms.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds an exception for joint ownership of improvements/derivative works. This directly contradicts the initial statement that *all* confidential information remains the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party in Section 4.1. Additionally, the second part of the change which allows the possibility of a separate licensing agreement between the parties creates an in-text contradiction by allowing future right or license to the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party, violating the original statement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information (including but not limited to all intellectual property rights emanating from or related to Confidential Information) remains the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party, except for any improvements or derivative works created by the Receiving Party using the Confidential Information, which shall be jointly owned. Nothing in this Agreement grants any right or license in the Confidential Information to the Receiving Party, unless a separate agreement is reached regarding licensing terms.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original clause stating that 'all confidential information remains the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party.' It introduces exceptions by specifying that improvements or derivative works created by the Receiving Party are jointly owned and that separate licensing agreements are possible, contradicting the initial exclusivity. This directly conflicts with the original intent, which stated that nothing in the agreement grants any right or license to the Receiving Party and that all Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party. Because the Contradicted text is the same as the Changed Text, the in-text contradiction remains. This aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in force and effect for [one (1)] year when duly signed by both Parties, provided, however that the confidentiality obligations imposed on the Receiving sid 4:4 Party under this Agreement shall continue for a period of [five (5)] years after termination of the Agreement, except to the extent this Agreement is superseded by stipulations of a contemplated agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in force and effect for [one (1)] year when duly signed by both Parties. Confidentiality obligations imposed on the Receiving sid 4:4 Party under this Agreement shall cease immediately upon termination of the Agreement, unless extended by mutual written agreement. Except to the extent this Agreement is superseded by stipulations of a contemplated agreement, the provisions of this Agreement are final.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement stated that confidentiality obligations would last five years after termination. The modified text says those obligations cease immediately upon termination, unless extended. This creates an in-text contradiction in the same section, 6.1, about the duration of confidentiality obligations after the agreement ends, since it can't be guaranteed that the parties agree after to the termination of the agreement. The added "the provisions of this Agreement are final" makes this even more contradictory, since it creates ambiguity on how this agreement is affected after the end date.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall remain in force and effect for [one (1)] year when duly signed by both Parties. Confidentiality obligations imposed on the Receiving sid 4:4 Party under this Agreement shall cease immediately upon termination of the Agreement, unless extended by mutual written agreement. Except to the extent this Agreement is superseded by stipulations of a contemplated agreement, the provisions of this Agreement are final.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulated that confidentiality obligations would extend for five years post-termination. The changed text states that these obligations cease immediately upon termination (unless extended), directly contradicting the previous five-year extension. The added clause about the provisions being final further exacerbates the contradiction, creating significant ambiguity about the actual duration of confidentiality obligations. This meets the definition of a Structural Flaw - In Text Contradiction since the clause is placed in the same section with conflicting implications, creating uncertainty about the obligations and rights of each party, and the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction, since different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties.
